name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَىٰ تِسْعَ ءَايَـٰتٍۭ بَيِّنَـٰتٍ ۖ فَسْـَٔلْ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ إِذْ جَآءَهُمْ فَقَالَ لَهُۥ فِرْعَوْنُ إِنِّى لَأَظُنُّكَ يَـٰمُوسَىٰ مَسْحُورًا (١٠١)
```

```english
Certainly We gave Moses nine manifest signs. So ask the Children of Israel. When he came to them, Pharaoh said to him, ‘O Moses, indeed I think you are bewitched.’ (101)
```

```arabic
قَالَ لَقَدْ عَلِمْتَ مَآ أَنزَلَ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ إِلَّا رَبُّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ بَصَآئِرَ وَإِنِّى لَأَظُنُّكَ يَـٰفِرْعَوْنُ مَثْبُورًا (١٠٢)
```

```english
He said, ‘You certainly know that no one has sent these [signs] except the Lord of the heavens and the earth, as eye-openers, and I, O Pharaoh, indeed think you are doomed.’ (102)
```

```arabic
فَأَرَادَ أَن يَسْتَفِزَّهُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ فَأَغْرَقْنَـٰهُ وَمَن مَّعَهُۥ جَمِيعًا (١٠٣)
```

```english
He desired to exterminate them from the land, so We drowned him and all those who were with him. (103)
```

```arabic
وَقُلْنَا مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ لِبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ ٱسْكُنُوا۟ ٱلْأَرْضَ فَإِذَا جَآءَ وَعْدُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ جِئْنَا بِكُمْ لَفِيفًا (١٠٤)
```

```english
After him We said to the Children of Israel, ‘Take up residence in the land, and when the occasion of the other [promise] comes, We shall gather you in mixed company.’ (104)
```

```arabic
وَبِٱلْحَقِّ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ وَبِٱلْحَقِّ نَزَلَ ۗ وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ إِلَّا مُبَشِّرًا وَنَذِيرًا (١٠٥)
```

```english
With the truth did We send it down, and with the truth did it descend, and We did not send you except as a bearer of good news and as a warner. (105)
```

```arabic
وَقُرْءَانًا فَرَقْنَـٰهُ لِتَقْرَأَهُۥ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ عَلَىٰ مُكْثٍ وَنَزَّلْنَـٰهُ تَنزِيلًا (١٠٦)
```

```english
We have sent the Quran in [discrete] parts so that you may recite it for the people a little at a time, and We have sent it down piecemeal. (106)
```

```arabic
قُلْ ءَامِنُوا۟ بِهِۦٓ أَوْ لَا تُؤْمِنُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْعِلْمَ مِن قَبْلِهِۦٓ إِذَا يُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ يَخِرُّونَ لِلْأَذْقَانِ سُجَّدًا (١٠٧)
```

```english
Say, ‘Whether you believe in it, or do not believe in it, indeed when it is recited to those who were given knowledge before it, they fall down in prostration on their faces, (107)
```

```arabic
وَيَقُولُونَ سُبْحَـٰنَ رَبِّنَآ إِن كَانَ وَعْدُ رَبِّنَا لَمَفْعُولًا (١٠٨)
```

```english
and say, ‘‘Immaculate is our Lord! Indeed Our Lord’s promise is bound to be fulfilled.’’ (108)
```

```arabic
وَيَخِرُّونَ لِلْأَذْقَانِ يَبْكُونَ وَيَزِيدُهُمْ خُشُوعًا ۩ (١٠٩)
```

```english
Weeping, they fall down on their faces, and it increases them in humility.’ (109)
```

```arabic
قُلِ ٱدْعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ أَوِ ٱدْعُوا۟ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنَ ۖ أَيًّا مَّا تَدْعُوا۟ فَلَهُ ٱلْأَسْمَآءُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ ۚ وَلَا تَجْهَرْ بِصَلَاتِكَ وَلَا تُخَافِتْ بِهَا وَٱبْتَغِ بَيْنَ ذَٰلِكَ سَبِيلًا (١١٠)
```

```english
Say, ‘Invoke ‘‘Allah’’ or invoke ‘‘the All-beneficent.’’ Whichever [of His Names] you may invoke, to Him belong the Best Names.’ Be neither loud in your prayer, nor murmur it, but follow a middle course between these, (110)
```

```arabic
وَقُلِ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِى لَمْ يَتَّخِذْ وَلَدًا وَلَمْ يَكُن لَّهُۥ شَرِيكٌ فِى ٱلْمُلْكِ وَلَمْ يَكُن لَّهُۥ وَلِىٌّ مِّنَ ٱلذُّلِّ ۖ وَكَبِّرْهُ تَكْبِيرًۢا (١١١)
```

```english
and say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, who has neither taken any son, nor has He any partner in sovereignty, nor has He any wali out of weakness,’ and magnify Him with a magnification [worthy of Him]. (111)
```

## (Commentary)

In these verses, there is a drawing of a parallel between the miracle of prophethood brought by the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—which is the Quran—and the polytheists' (المشركين) turning away from it and their arbitrary proposal of other signs (آيات), and that which Moses (عليه‌السلام) brought of the signs of prophethood and Pharaoh’s turning away from them, accusing him of being bewitched (مسحور). Then, the discourse returns to describing the Quran, the reason for its gradual revelation in separate parts, and the divine knowledge (المعارف) associated with it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنا مُوسى تِسْعَ آياتٍ بَيِّناتٍ فَسْئَلْ بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ إِذْ جاءَهُمْ فَقالَ لَهُ فِرْعَوْنُ إِنِّي لَأَظُنُّكَ يا مُوسى مَسْحُوراً*» — **Certainly We gave Moses nine manifest signs. So ask the Children of Israel when he came to them, and Pharaoh said to him, ‘O Moses, I indeed think you are bewitched.’** (17:101). The signs given to Moses (عليه‌السلام) according to what the Quran narrates are more than nine; however, the signs he brought specifically for the mission to Pharaoh, as mentioned in the Quran, are nine. They are: the staff (العصا), the hand (اليد), the flood (الطوفان), the locusts (الجراد), the lice (القمل), the frogs (الضفدع), the blood (الدم), the years of drought (السنون), and the scarcity of fruits (نقص من الثمرات). It is apparent that these are what is intended by the "nine signs" mentioned in the verse, especially given what is narrated of Moses' speech to Pharaoh: «*لَقَدْ عَلِمْتَ ما أَنْزَلَ هؤُلاءِ إِلَّا رَبُّ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ بَصائِرَ*» — **He said, ‘You certainly know that no one has sent these down except the Lord of the heavens and the earth as eye-openers.’** (17:102). As for signs other than these—such as the parting of the sea (البحر), the rock (الحجر), the reviving of the murdered man by the cow (إحياء المقتول بالبقرة), the reviving of those struck by the thunderbolt (الصاعقة) from his people, the raising of the mountain (نتق الجبل) above them, and others—they fall outside of these nine mentioned in the verse.

This is not contradicted by the fact that these signs appeared gradually, for this dialogue is extracted from the sum total of the disputation between Moses and Pharaoh throughout the duration of his mission.

Therefore, no weight is given to what some exegetes have mentioned in opposition to what we have enumerated, as there is no evidence for it. In the Torah, it is mentioned that the nine are: the staff, the blood, the frogs, the lice, the death of livestock, hail like fire sent down with a blazing fire that destroyed the plants and animals it passed, the locusts, the darkness, and the death of the firstborn of humans and all animals.

Perhaps the discrepancy between the Torah and the apparent meaning of the Quran regarding the nine signs is what necessitated the omission of the detailed list of the nine signs in the verse, so that the command to ask the Jews would remain valid; for despite the explicit contradiction, they would not have believed the Quran, but would have hastened to denial (التكذيب) before affirmation (التصديق).

And His saying: «*إِنِّي لَأَظُنُّكَ يا مُوسى مَسْحُوراً*» — **‘I indeed think you are bewitched’** means: you have been affected by sorcery so your intellect has become disordered. This is similar in meaning to his saying narrated in another place: «*إِنَّ رَسُولَكُمُ الَّذِي أُرْسِلَ إِلَيْكُمْ لَمَجْنُونٌ*» — **‘Indeed your apostle who has been sent to you is surely crazy.’** (26:27). It has also been said that "bewitched" (المسحور) means the sorcerer (الساحر), similar to *al-maymun* (الميمون) and *al-mash’um* (المشئوم) meaning the one who brings good or bad fortune; its origin is the use of the passive participle form for attribution. The meaning of the verse is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*قالَ لَقَدْ عَلِمْتَ ما أَنْزَلَ هؤُلاءِ إِلَّا رَبُّ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ بَصائِرَ وَإِنِّي لَأَظُنُّكَ يا فِرْعَوْنُ مَثْبُوراً*» — **He said, ‘You certainly know that no one has sent these down except the Lord of the heavens and the earth as eye-openers, and I indeed think you are doomed, O Pharaoh.’** (17:102). **The doomed** (المثبور) is the one who is destroyed, derived from *al-thubur* (الثبور) meaning destruction (الهلاك). The meaning is: Moses said, addressing Pharaoh: You certainly know, O Pharaoh, that no one sent down these manifest signs except the Lord of the heavens and the earth; He sent them as eye-openers (بَصائِرَ) — *clear proofs by which one gains insight to distinguish truth from falsehood* — and I indeed think you, O Pharaoh, are doomed to destruction in the Hereafter due to your obstinacy and denial.

He used the word "think" (الظن) rather than "certainty" (اليقين) because judgment belongs to Allah, and to correspond with Pharaoh's words: «*إِنِّي لَأَظُنُّكَ يا مُوسى*» — **‘I indeed think you, O Moses...’** etc. Furthermore, "thinking" (الظن) is sometimes used in contexts of certainty.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*فَأَرادَ أَنْ يَسْتَفِزَّهُمْ مِنَ الْأَرْضِ فَأَغْرَقْناهُ وَمَنْ مَعَهُ جَمِيعاً*» — **So he desired to unsettle them from the land, but We drowned him and those who were with him, all together.** (17:103). **Unsettling** (الاستفزاز) is to disturb and expel with violence. The meaning of the verse is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*وَقُلْنا مِنْ بَعْدِهِ لِبَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ اسْكُنُوا الْأَرْضَ فَإِذا جاءَ وَعْدُ الْآخِرَةِ جِئْنا بِكُمْ لَفِيفاً*» — **After him We said to the Children of Israel, ‘Settle in the land, and when the promise of the Hereafter comes to pass, We will bring you as a mixed crowd.’** (17:104). The "land" they were commanded to settle in is the Holy Land (الأرض المقدسة) which Allah had ordained for them, as evidenced by His saying: «*ادْخُلُوا الْأَرْضَ الْمُقَدَّسَةَ الَّتِي كَتَبَ اللهُ لَكُمْ*» — **‘Enter the Holy Land which Allah has ordained for you’** (5:21), and other verses. Similarly, the "land" in the previous verse refers to the land in general or the land of Egypt, as evidenced by the context (السياق).

And His saying: «*فَإِذا جاءَ وَعْدُ الْآخِرَةِ*» — **‘and when the promise of the Hereafter comes to pass’** means the promise of the second turn or the life of the Hereafter. According to what the exegetes have mentioned, it refers to the Day of Resurrection. And His saying: «*جِئْنا بِكُمْ لَفِيفاً*» — **‘We will bring you as a mixed crowd’** means gathered together, entwined with one another.

The meaning is: We said to the Children of Israel after the drowning of Pharaoh: Settle in the Holy Land—whereas Pharaoh had wanted to unsettle them from the land—and when the Day of Resurrection arrives, We shall bring you all together, entwined, for the reckoning and the final judgment.

It is not far-fetched that the "promise of the Hereafter" (وعد الآخرة) refers to what Allah, the Glorified, mentioned at the beginning of the Surah regarding His decree to the Children of Israel: «*فَإِذا جاءَ وَعْدُ الْآخِرَةِ لِيَسُوؤُا وُجُوهَكُمْ وَلِيَدْخُلُوا الْمَسْجِدَ كَما دَخَلُوهُ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ وَلِيُتَبِّرُوا ما عَلَوْا تَتْبِيراً*» — **‘So when the second promise came to pass, [We sent them] to sadden your faces and to enter the Temple just as they had entered it the first time, and to destroy utterly whatever they conquered.’** (17:7). Although the majority of exegetes did not mention this, it would link the conclusion of the Surah back to its beginning. The meaning would then be: We commanded them after the drowning of Pharaoh to settle in the Holy Land from which Pharaoh was preventing you, and remain therein until the second promise comes, in which affliction shall envelop you through killing, captivity, and exile; then We shall gather you from it and bring you as a mixed crowd—referring to their captivity and exile to Babylon.

On this interpretation, the subtle point (النكتة) of branching His saying: «*فَإِذا جاءَ وَعْدُ الْآخِرَةِ*» etc., from His saying: «*وَقُلْنا مِنْ بَعْدِهِ لِبَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ اسْكُنُوا الْأَرْضَ*» becomes clear, unlike the previous interpretation where no obvious subtle point results from this branching.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*وَبِالْحَقِّ أَنْزَلْناهُ وَبِالْحَقِّ نَزَلَ وَما أَرْسَلْناكَ إِلَّا مُبَشِّراً وَنَذِيراً*» — **With the truth have We sent it down, and with the truth has it descended, and We did not send you except as a bearer of good news and as a warner.** (17:105). Having finished the parallel, He returns to explaining the state of the Quran and mentioning its attributes. He mentions that He sent it down in a manner accompanied by the truth (الحق), and it descended from Him in a manner accompanied by the truth. Thus, it is protected from falsehood (الباطل) from the direction of the One who sent it down; it is not idle or nonsensical speech, nor has anything entered it that could ever corrupt it, nor has anyone shared in it such that they might abrogate it at any time. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is nothing but a messenger from Him, the Exalted, bringing good news and warning with it. He has no right to manipulate it by addition or omission, nor to abandon it entirely or partially based on the people's suggestions or his own desire, nor to turn away from it to ask Allah for another sign according to his desire or the people's desire, nor to compromise with them regarding it or be lenient in any of its divine knowledge or laws. All of this is because it is truth (حق) originating from a source of truth, and what is there after truth except error (الضلال)?

Thus, His saying: «*وَما أَرْسَلْناكَ*» etc., completes the preceding discourse. Its summary is that the Quran is a true sign (آية حقة), and no one has the right to manipulate it in any way; the Prophet and others are equal in this regard.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*وَقُرْآناً فَرَقْناهُ لِتَقْرَأَهُ عَلَى النَّاسِ عَلى مُكْثٍ وَنَزَّلْناهُ تَنْزِيلاً*» — **A Quran which We have unfolded in parts so that you may read it to the people at intervals, and We have sent it down in successive revelations.** (17:106). This is conjoined to what preceded it; meaning: We sent it down with the truth and We unfolded it as a Quran. It is said in *al-Majma‘*: The meaning of "We unfolded it" (فرقناه) is that We separated it and sent it down verse by verse and Surah by Surah. This is indicated by His saying: «*عَلى مُكْثٍ*» — **‘at intervals’**. *Al-mukth* (المكث) can be pronounced with a *damma* or a *fatha* on the *mim*; they are two linguistic variants. [End of quote].

The term itself encompasses the descent of the Quranic knowledge (المعارف)—which is with Allah—into the mold of words and expressions that can only be received gradually and cannot be engaged with except with intervals and deliberation, so that it is easier for people to intellectually grasp and memorize it, as in His saying: «*إِنَّا جَعَلْناهُ قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ وَإِنَّهُ فِي أُمِّ الْكِتابِ لَدَيْنا لَعَلِيٌّ حَكِيمٌ*» — **‘We have made it an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason. And indeed it is with Us in the Mother of the Book, sublime and wise.’** (43:3-4).

It also encompasses the descent of the Quranic verses in separate installments (نجوما مفرقة), Surah by Surah and verse by verse, according to the people's progress in their readiness to receive the fundamental knowledge for belief (الاعتقاد) and the secondary laws for action (العمل), and as dictated by the public interests (المصالح). This was so that knowledge would be paired with action, and so that human nature would not recoil from it, by taking its knowledge and laws one after another, as would have happened had it been sent down all at once. The Torah was sent down all at once, and the Jews did not receive it with acceptance until the mountain was raised (نتق الجبل) above them as if it were a canopy.

However, what is more consistent with the context of the previous verses—which included their narrated proposal: «*حَتَّى تُنَزِّلَ عَلَيْنا كِتاباً نَقْرَؤُهُ*» — **‘...until you bring down to us a book that we may read’** (17:93), which clearly suggests a proposal for the Quran to be sent down all at once—is that the "unfolding" (تفريق) of the Quran refers to its descent Surah by Surah and verse by verse according to the gradual occurrence of the occasions of revelation (أسباب النزول). People repeatedly proposed that the Quran be sent down as a single whole, as in: «*وَقالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَوْ لا نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ الْقُرْآنُ جُمْلَةً واحِدَةً*» — **‘The faithless say, ‘Why was not the Quran sent down to him all at once?’’** (25:32), and His saying narrating from the People of the Book: «*يَسْئَلُكَ أَهْلُ الْكِتابِ أَنْ تُنَزِّلَ عَلَيْهِمْ كِتاباً مِنَ السَّماءِ*» — **‘The People of the Book ask you to bring down for them a Book from the sky.’** (4:153).

This is supported by the conclusion of the verse with His saying: «*وَنَزَّلْناهُ تَنْزِيلاً*» — **‘and We have sent it down in successive revelations’**, for *al-tanzil* (التنزيل)—which is the gradual sending down of something—is more closely related to the second consideration than the first.

Nevertheless, the second consideration—which is the detailing and unfolding of the Quran according to its descent by sending down some of it after others without it being sent down all at once—necessitates the first consideration, which is its detailing and unfolding into various followed items of knowledge and laws after they were all integrated into a single reality, folded and gathered in their roots within a single, unique origin.

For this reason, Allah, the Glorified, detailed His Book into Surahs and verses after He clothed it in the garment of Arabic wording, so that it would be easy for people to understand it, as He said: «*لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ*» — **‘so that you may exercise your reason’**. Then He categorized it into types and arranged it in an order, sending it down one after another when the need arose and according to the attainment of the people's varying readiness and the completion of their receptivity for each part. This took place over a full twenty-three years, so that instruction would be accompanied by spiritual training (التربية), and knowledge would be paired with action.

We shall return to the discussion of some matters related to the verse and the Surah in a separate discourse, Allah willing.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*قُلْ آمِنُوا بِهِ أَوْ لا تُؤْمِنُوا*» — **Say, ‘Believe in it, or do not believe.’** (17:107) to the end of the three verses. What is intended by "those who were given knowledge before it" (الذين أوتوا العلم من قبله) are those who attained realization through knowledge of Allah and His signs before the descent of the Quran, whether they were Jews, Christians, or others. There is no reason for restriction, unless it is said that the context implies these were people of truth, and the religion that was not abrogated at that time was the religion of the Messiah (عليه‌السلام); thus, they would be the people of truth among the Christian scholars who did not deviate or change [the scripture].

In any case, the meaning of their being "given knowledge before it" is that they were prepared to understand the word of truth and accept it because they were equipped with knowledge of the reality of its meaning, which bequeathed to them the attribute of humility (الخشوع); thus, the Quran recited to them increases them in humility.

And His saying: «*يَخِرُّونَ لِلْأَذْقانِ سُجَّداً*» — **‘they fall down on their faces in prostration’**. **Faces** (الأذقان) is the plural of *dhaqan* (ذقن), which is the chin where the two jawbones meet. **Falling down** (الخرور) on the chins means falling to the ground on their faces for prostration, as explained by His saying: «*سُجَّداً*» — **‘in prostration’**. The chins were specified because the chin is the part of the face closest to the ground when falling upon it for prostration. It is sometimes said that "chins" refers to the faces, using the part to represent the whole metaphorically.

And His saying: «*وَيَقُولُونَ سُبْحانَ رَبِّنا إِنْ كانَ وَعْدُ رَبِّنا لَمَفْعُولاً*» — **‘and say, ‘Immaculate is our Lord! Indeed our Lord’s promise was bound to be fulfilled.’’** (17:108). That is, they glorify Him, the Exalted, from every deficiency and specifically from breaking a promise. The context of the previous verses indicates that the "promise" refers to His promise of the Resurrection (البعث), and this is in contrast to the polytheists' insistence on denying the Resurrection and the Return (المعاد), as repeated in the previous verses.

And His saying: «*وَيَخِرُّونَ لِلْأَذْقانِ يَبْكُونَ وَيَزِيدُهُمْ خُشُوعاً*» — **‘They fall down on their faces weeping, and it increases them in humility.’** (17:109). The repetition of "falling down on their faces" and its association with weeping is to convey the meaning of **submission** (الخضوع) — *the humility expressed through the body* — just as the second clause conveys the meaning of **humility** (الخشوع) — *the humility that resides in the heart*. The summary of the verse is that they submit and are humble.

In the verse is the affirmation of the characteristic of the believers for them, which was referred to previously by His saying: «*وَنُنَزِّلُ مِنَ الْقُرْآنُ ما هُوَ شِفاءٌ وَرَحْمَةٌ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ*» — **‘We send down in the Quran that which is a cure and mercy for the believers’** (17:82). Similarly, the verse negates the characteristic of the polytheists from them, which is the denial of the Resurrection.

In these three verses is an explanation that the Quran is independent of their faith; not because the faith of those given knowledge before it removes the need for the faith of others, but because their faith in it reveals that it is a Book of truth sent down with the truth. It has no need for its truthfulness, nor any lack in its perfection, for the faith of any believer or the affirmation of any affirmer. If they believe in it, it is for their own souls, and if they disbelieve in it, it is against them; it is neither for it nor against it.

He, the Glorified, mentioned their turning away from the Quran, their disbelief in it, their lack of regard for it being a sign, and their proposal of other signs. Then He explained for it such attributes of perfection and evidences of inimitability (الإعجاز) in its wording and meaning, and its profound effect on souls, and the manner of its descent, through which it became clear that it is truth which no falsehood or corruption can ever touch. Then He explained in these verses that it is independent of their faith; they are left to what they choose of faith or disbelief.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*قُلِ ادْعُوا اللهَ أَوِ ادْعُوا الرَّحْمنَ أَيًّا ما تَدْعُوا فَلَهُ الْأَسْماءُ الْحُسْنى*» — **Say, ‘Invoke ‘‘Allah’’ or invoke ‘‘the All-beneficent.’’ Whichever [of His names] you may invoke, to Him belong the Best Names.’** (17:110). The word "or" (أو) is for parity and permissibility. What is intended by "Allah" and "the All-Beneficent" (الرحمن) are the two names indicating the Named, rather than the Named Himself. The meaning is: Invoke by the name of Allah or by the name of the All-Beneficent, for the invocation is an invocation of Him.

And His saying: «*أَيًّا ما تَدْعُوا*» — **‘Whichever you may invoke’** is a conditional clause, and "what" (ما) is a connective for emphasis, similar to His saying: «*فَبِما رَحْمَةٍ مِنَ اللهِ*» — **‘It is by Allah’s mercy...’** (3:159) and His saying: «*عَمَّا قَلِيلٍ لَيُصْبِحُنَّ نادِمِينَ*» — **‘In a little while they will surely become regretful.’** (23:40). "Whichever" (أَيًّا) is the conditional particle and is the object of "you invoke" (تَدْعُوا).

And His saying: «*فَلَهُ الْأَسْماءُ الْحُسْنى*» — **‘to Him belong the Best Names’** is the response to the condition (جواب الشرط). It is an instance of placing the cause in the position of the effect. The meaning is: Whichever of the two names you invoke, it is a most beautiful name belonging to Him, because all the Best Names (الأسماء الحسنى) belong to Him. The names indicating the named entities include those that are beautiful (حسنة), indicating what contains beauty, and those that are ugly (قبيحة), which are the opposite; there is no way for the ugly to be attributed to Him, the Exalted. Among the beautiful names are those that are "most beautiful" (أحسن), containing no taint of deficiency or ugliness, such as Wealth (الغنى) with which there is no poverty, Life (الحياة) with which there is no death, and Might (العزة) with which there is no humiliation. Others are beautiful where beauty predominates without being pure. To Allah, the Glorified, belong the Best Names (الأسماء الحسنى) — *every name that is the most beautiful of names in its meaning* — as indicated by the saying of the leaders of the religion: "Indeed Allah, the Exalted, is Wealthy but not like the wealthy, Living but not like the living, Mighty but not like the mighty, Knowing but not like the knowing," and so on. That is, He possesses the pure and absolute form of every perfection, untainted by its opposite.

The pronoun in His saying: «*أَيًّا ما تَدْعُوا فَلَهُ الْأَسْماءُ الْحُسْنى*» refers to the Essence (الذات) which transcends every name and description. It does not refer back to either of the two names, "Allah" or "the All-Beneficent," because what is intended by them—as mentioned—are the names rather than the transcendent Essence which is named by them. It would make no sense to say: Whichever of the two names you invoke, that name possesses all the Best Names or the rest of the Best Names. Rather, the meaning is: Whichever of His names you invoke, there is no barrier to it, because they are all His names, for they are Best Names and to Him belong the Best Names. They are the paths to invoking Him, and invoking them is invoking Him, for they are His names, and the name is the mirror of the Named and His title; so understand that.

The verse is among the most brilliant of Quranic verses, illuminating the reality of what the Holy Quran holds regarding the monotheism of the Essence (توحيد الذات) and the monotheism of worship (توحيد العبادة), in contrast to what the idolaters (الوثنية) hold regarding the monotheism of the Essence while associating partners in worship (تشريك العبادة).

For the idolaters—as a summary of their views was presented in the tenth volume of this book—believe that He, the Glorified, is an Essence transcending every limit and description, who then became determined by names, one name after another, and they call this "procreation" (تولدا). They believe that the angels and jinn are high manifestations (مظاهر) of His names, so they are His children who manage the universe. They believe that the worship of the worshippers and the orientation of those who orient themselves does not exceed the level of the names, nor does it surpass the rank of the "children" who are the manifestations of His names. For in what we worship, we only worship the God (الإله), or the Creator (الخالق), or the Provider (الرازق), or the Giver of Life (المحيي), or the Bringer of Death (المميت), and so on. All of these are names whose manifestations are the "children" from among the angels and jinn. As for the transcendent Essence, It is too high to be reached by sense, imagination, or intellect, and too exalted for any orientation, seeking, worship, or ritual to attach to It.

Thus, according to them, the invocation of every name is the worship of that name—meaning the angel or jinn who is the manifestation of that name—and he is the god (الإله) worshipped through that worship. Consequently, the gods multiply with the multiplication of the types of invocations for various needs. For this reason, when some of the polytheists heard the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) praying in his prayer: "O Allah, O All-Beneficent," they said: "Look at this Sabian; he forbids us from worshipping two gods while he himself invokes two gods."

The noble verse refutes them and reveals the error in their view by stating that these names are Best Names belonging to Him, the Exalted. They are purely owned by Him, having no independence from Him in description, nor are they separate from Him in essence or attribute; He owns them and they subsist through Him. They possess nothing but pure "path-ness" (الطريقية). Thus, invoking them is invoking Him, and orienting through them is orienting toward Him. How can it be correct for the name to veil the Named when it is nothing but a path indicating Him, a guide leading to Him, and a face for Him through which He manifests to others? Therefore, the invocation of many names does not contradict the monotheism of the worship of the Essence, just as it is impossible for worship to stop at the name and not exceed it.

From this explanation, the error in counting the names or their manifestations from among the angels and jinn as "children" of Him, the Exalted, becomes apparent. For the application of "offspring" (الولد) and "child" (الابن)—whether literally or as an honorific—necessitates a type of homogeneity and partnership between the child and the parent in the reality of the essence or in one of its perfections. The courtyard of His majesty is sanctified from anything else sharing with Him in essence or perfection; for what He possesses is for Himself, and what others possess is His, not for themselves.

Likewise, the error in attributing the management of the universe in its various forms to them becomes apparent. For these angels, as well as the names of which they are manifestations according to them, do not own anything for themselves, nor are they independent of Him in anything. Rather, they are honored servants; they do not precede Him in speech, and they act by His command. The same applies to the jinn in what they do. In short, there is no cause among the active causes in the universe except that He, the Exalted, is the one who granted it power over what it does; He is the Owner of what He has granted, and the One powerful over that which He has empowered it.

This is what the following verse conveys: «*وَقُلِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي لَمْ يَتَّخِذْ وَلَداً وَلَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُ شَرِيكٌ فِي الْمُلْكِ وَلَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُ وَلِيٌّ مِنَ الذُّلِ*» — **And say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, who has not taken any son, nor has He any partner in sovereignty, nor has He any ally [to help Him] out of weakness.’** (17:111). We shall repeat the reference to this, Allah willing.

In the verse is an indication that the word of majesty (لفظ الجلالة) — *Allah* — is among the Best Names. In its origin—*al-ilah* (الإله)—it is a description conveying the meaning of "being worshipped" (المعبودية), even if it became a proper noun (العلمية) through frequent usage. This is indicated by the correctness of applying attributes to it; one says: "Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful," but one does not say: "The All-Beneficent, Allah, the Most Merciful." In His speech, the Exalted: «*بِسْمِ اللهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ*» — **In the Name of Allah, the All-beneficent, the All-merciful.** (1:1).

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*وَلا تَجْهَرْ بِصَلاتِكَ وَلا تُخافِتْ بِها وَابْتَغِ بَيْنَ ذلِكَ سَبِيلاً*» — **Do not be loud-voiced in your prayer, nor be low-voiced in it, and seek a way in between.** (17:110). Loudness (الجهر) and whispering (الإخفات) are relative attributes that characterize sounds. Sometimes a third quality is considered between them, which is whispering relative to loudness and loudness relative to whispering. Thus, **loudness** (الجهر) is exaggeration in raising the voice, and **whispering** (الإخفات) is exaggeration in lowering it, and what is between them is moderation. The meaning of the verse would then be: Do not exaggerate in your prayer in loudness nor in whispering, but rather follow a way between them, which is moderation. It is called a "way" (سبيلا) because it is a practice (سنة) to be followed by him and those of his community of believers who emulate him.

This is if the "prayer" in His saying: «*بِصَلاتِكَ*» — **‘in your prayer’** is intended to be exhaustive, referring to every single prayer. However, if the collective whole is intended—and this is perhaps more apparent—the meaning would be: Do not be loud in all your prayers, nor be low-voiced in all of them, but rather take a middle way, being loud in some and low-voiced in others. This meaning is more appropriate in view of what is established in the Sunnah regarding loudness in some of the daily obligatory prayers, such as Dawn (الصبح), Sunset (المغرب), and Night (العشاء), and whispering in others.

Perhaps this interpretation is more fitting in view of the connection between the end of the verse and its beginning. Loudness in prayer suits His being High and Transcendent (علي متعال), and whispering suits His being Near (قريب), closer than the jugular vein. Thus, adopting both qualities in the prayers is a fulfillment of the right of all His names.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*وَقُلِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي لَمْ يَتَّخِذْ وَلَداً وَلَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُ شَرِيكٌ فِي الْمُلْكِ وَلَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُ وَلِيٌّ مِنَ الذُّلِّ وَكَبِّرْهُ تَكْبِيراً*» — **And say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, who has not taken any son, nor has He any partner in sovereignty, nor has He any ally [to help Him] out of weakness,’ and magnify Him with a [worthy] magnification.** (17:111). This is conjoined to His saying in the previous verse: «*قُلِ ادْعُوا اللهَ أَوِ ادْعُوا الرَّحْمنَ*». The summary of the discourse returns to: Say to them that these names you invoke and claim to be worshipped gods besides Him are only His names; they are owned by Him, owning neither themselves nor anything for themselves. Thus, invoking them is invoking Him, for He is the Worshipped in every case.

Then, praise Him and laud Him for what results from the absolute nature of His sovereignty (ملك). For nothing resembles Him in essence or attribute such that it could be a son to Him—if it were derived from Him in essence or attribute, as the idolaters and the People of the Book among the Christians and Jews, and the ancient Magians say regarding the angels, the jinn, the Messiah, Ezra, or the rabbis. Nor can there be a partner (شريك) if it shared in sovereignty without derivation, as the idolaters, dualists (الثنويون), and others among the worshippers of Satan say. Nor can there be an ally (ولي) for Him if it shared in sovereignty and surpassed Him, thus rectifying some part of His kingdom that He was unable to rectify.

In another way: nothing is of His genus such that it could be a son if it were below Him, or a partner if it were equal to Him in rank, or an ally if it were superior to Him in sovereignty.

The verse is, in reality, a laudation of Him, the Exalted, for His absolute sovereignty, from which follows the negation of the son, the partner, and the ally. For this reason, He commanded him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to use praise (التحميد) rather than glorification (التسبيح), even though the negation of the son, partner, and ally mentioned therein are negative attributes (صفات سلبية) for which glorification is more suitable than praise; so understand that.

He, the Glorified, concluded the verse with His saying: «*وَكَبِّرْهُ تَكْبِيراً*» — **‘and magnify Him with a [worthy] magnification.’** This magnification is left absolute after the description and sanctification; thus, it is a magnification beyond every description. Therefore, "Allah is Greater" (الله أكبر) has been interpreted as meaning He is greater than can be described, as narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام). If the meaning were that He is greater than everything, it would not be free from associating things with Him, the Exalted, in the meaning of greatness, while His courtyard is too mighty for anything to share with Him in any matter.

Among the subtle beauties of the Surah is the opening of its first verse with glorification (التسبيح) and the conclusion of its last verse with magnification (التكبير), while also opening it with praise (التحميد).

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Sa‘id ibn Mansur, ibn al-Mundhir, and ibn Abi Hatim narrated from ‘Ali that he used to read: *laqad ‘alimta* (لقد علمتَ) — meaning with the *fatha* [on the *ta*]. ‘Ali said: "By Allah, the enemy of Allah [Pharaoh] did not know, but it was Moses who knew."

**I say:** This is a recitation attributed to him (عليه‌السلام).

In *al-Kafi*, from ‘Ali ibn Muhammad with his chain of transmission, he said: Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) was asked about someone who has an ailment on his forehead and cannot prostrate upon it. He said: "**يضع ذقنه على الأرض إن الله عز وجل يقول ـ يخرُّون للأذقان سجداً**" — "He places his chin on the ground, for Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: ‘they fall down on their faces (chins) in prostration.’"

**I say:** There are other narrations with the same meaning.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, ibn Jarir and ibn Mardawayh narrated from ibn ‘Abbas who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) prayed in Mecca one day and invoked Allah. He said in his invocation: "O Allah, O All-Beneficent." The polytheists said: "Look at this Sabian; he forbids us from invoking two gods while he himself invokes two gods." So Allah sent down: «*قُلِ ادْعُوا اللهَ أَوِ ادْعُوا الرَّحْمنَ*» the verse.

**I say:** There are other narrations regarding the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) for this verse that differ from this one and mention other things; however, this narration is the closest in its correspondence to the content of the verse.

In *al-Tawhid*, with a chain of transmission, and in *al-Ihtijaj*, as a *mursal* narration, from Hisham ibn al-Hakam who said: I asked Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) about the names of Allah, the Mighty is His mention, and their derivation. I said: "From what is 'Allah' derived?" He said: "O Hisham, 'Allah' is derived from *ilah* (إله), and *ilah* necessitates a 'worshipped one' (مألوها). The name is other than the Named. Whoever worships the name without the meaning has disbelieved and has not worshipped anything. Whoever worships the name and the meaning has disbelieved and has worshipped two. Whoever worships the meaning without the name, that is monotheism (التوحيد). Do you understand, O Hisham?"

He said: I said: "Tell me more." He said: "Indeed, to Allah, Blessed and Exalted, belong ninety-nine names. If the name were the Named, every name among them would be a god. But Allah is a meaning indicated by these names, and all of them are other than Him. O Hisham, 'bread' is the name of the food, 'water' is the name of the drink, 'garment' is the name of the clothing, and 'fire' is the name of that which burns." [The tradition continues].

In *al-Tawhid*, with its chain of transmission from ibn Ri’ab from more than one person from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) who said: "**من عبد الله بالتوهم فقد كفر ، ومن عبد الاسم ولم يعبد المعنى فقد كفر ، ومن عبد الاسم والمعنى فقد أشرك ، ومن عبد المعنى بإيقاع الأسماء عليه بصفاته ـ التي يصف بها نفسه فعقد عليه قلبه ـ ونطق به لسانه في سرائره وعلانيته ـ فأولئك أصحاب أمير المؤمنين**" — "Whoever worships Allah through imagination has disbelieved. Whoever worships the name and does not worship the meaning has disbelieved. Whoever worships the name and the meaning has associated partners. Whoever worships the meaning by applying the names to It through His attributes—with which He describes Himself—and binds his heart to It, and his tongue speaks of It in his secrets and openly—those are the companions of the Commander of the Faithful." In another tradition: "Those are the true believers."

In the *Tawhid* of *al-Bihar*, in the chapter on the distinction between the name and the meaning, from *al-Tawhid* with its chain of transmission from Ibrahim ibn ‘Umar from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) who said: "Indeed, Allah, Blessed and Exalted, created a name with letters that are not described, with a word that is not spoken, with a person that is not embodied, with a likeness that is not characterized, and with a color that is not dyed. Dimensions are negated from it, limits are distanced from it, and the sense of every imaginer is veiled from it. It is hidden but not veiled. He made it a complete word consisting of four parts together—none of which is before the other. He manifested three things from it due to the need of the creation for them, and He veiled one of them—which is the Hidden, Treasured Name—by these three names that were manifested.

The manifested ones are: Allah, the Blessed (تبارك), and the Immaculate (سبحان). For each of these names there are four pillars, making twelve pillars. Then He created for each pillar thirty names and acts attributed to them. So He is the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful, the King, the Holy, the Creator, the Maker, the Shaper, the Living, the Self-Subsisting, whom neither slumber nor sleep overtakes, the All-Knowing, the All-Aware, the All-Hearing, the All-Seeing, the All-Wise, the All-Mighty, the All-Compelling, the All-Proud, the High, the Great, the All-Powerful, the Able, the Peace, the Faithful, the Guardian, the Maker, the Originator, the Incomparable, the Exalted, the Majestic, the Generous, the Provider, the Giver of Life, the Bringer of Death, the Resurrector, the Inheritor.

These names and whatever belongs to the Best Names—until they complete three hundred and sixty names—are attributions to these three names. These three names are pillars and veils for the One Name—the Hidden, Treasured one—veiled by these three names. That is His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: «*قُلِ ادْعُوا اللهَ أَوِ ادْعُوا الرَّحْمنَ ـ أَيًّا ما تَدْعُوا فَلَهُ الْأَسْماءُ الْحُسْنى*»."

**I say:** The tradition is also narrated in *al-Kafi* from him (عليه‌السلام).

It has already been mentioned in the discussion of the Best Names in the eighth volume of this book that these words called the names of Allah are only the "names of the names" (أسماء الأسماء). That which they indicate and point to of the referent—meaning the Essence—is taken with the description of what the name is according to reality. Accordingly, some of the Best Names are identical to the Essence (عين الذات), which are those comprising a positive perfection attribute such as the Living, the All-Knowing, and the All-Powerful. Others are additional to the Essence and outside of It, which are those comprising a negative or active attribute, such as the Creator, the Provider, and "neither slumber nor sleep overtakes Him." This is regarding the names. As for the "names of the names"—which are the words indicating the Essence taken with one of Its descriptions—there is no doubt that they are other than the Essence, and that they are originated words subsisting in the one who utters them.

However, there is a disagreement here from two perspectives:

First: Some ignorant people among the early theologians (متكلمي السلف) confused the names with the "names of the names." They thought that what was meant by the identity (عينية) of the names with the Essence was the identity of the "names of the names" with It. Thus, they held that the name is the Named, and accordingly, worshipping the name and invoking it would be identical to worshipping the Named. This view was prevalent in the early Abbasid era, and the two previous narrations—meaning the two narrations from *al-Tawhid*—are in refutation of it.

Second: What the idolaters hold, which is that Allah, the Glorified, is not the object of worshipful orientation; rather, it attaches to the names. Thus, the names or their manifestations from among the angels, jinn, and perfect humans are the ones invoked and are the worshipped gods besides Allah. You have already seen in the preceding commentary that His saying, the Exalted: «*قُلِ ادْعُوا اللهَ أَوِ ادْعُوا الرَّحْمنَ*» etc., is a refutation of this.

The last narration also reveals the manner of the names' emergence from the transcendent Essence, which is too high for knowledge to encompass, or for description and attribute to restrict, or for name and title to limit. Through the explanation in its beginning and end, it is explicit that what is meant by "names" therein are the names themselves, rather than the "names of the names." We have explained this to some extent in the conclusion of the discussion on the Best Names in the eighth volume of this book; so refer to it if you wish.

In the *Tafsir* of al-‘Ayyashi, from Zurarah, Humran, and Muhammad ibn Muslim from Abu Ja‘far and Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه‌السلام): regarding His saying, the Exalted: «*وَلا تَجْهَرْ بِصَلاتِكَ وَلا تُخافِتْ بِها وَابْتَغِ بَيْنَ ذلِكَ سَبِيلاً*». He said: "**كان رسول الله صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله إذا كان بمكة جهر بصوته ـ فيعلم بمكانه المشركون فكانوا يؤذونه ـ فأنزلت هذه الآية عند ذلك**" — "When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was in Mecca, he would be loud with his voice, so the polytheists would know his location and harm him. So this verse was sent down at that time."

**I say:** This meaning is narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from ibn Mardawayh from ibn ‘Abbas. It is also narrated from ‘A’ishah that it was sent down regarding invocation (الدعاء), and there is no harm in this as it does not contradict [the other]. It is also narrated from her that it was sent down regarding the *tashahhud*.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of transmission from Sama‘ah, he said: I asked him about the saying of Allah, the Exalted: «*وَلا تَجْهَرْ بِصَلاتِكَ وَلا تُخافِتْ بِها*». He said: "**المخافتة ما دون سمعك والجهر أن ترفع صوتك شديدا**" — "Whispering is what is below your own hearing, and loudness is to raise your voice intensely."

**I say:** In this is support for the first meaning mentioned in the exegesis of the verse.

In it, with its chain of transmission from ‘Abdullah ibn Sinan, he said: I said to Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه‌السلام): "Must the Imam make those behind him hear, even if they are many?" He said: "**ليقرأ وسطا يقول الله تبارك وتعالى : ولا تجهر بصلاتك ولا تخافت بها**" — "He should recite moderately; Allah, Blessed and Exalted, says: ‘Do not be loud-voiced in your prayer, nor be low-voiced in it.’"

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ahmad and al-Tabarani narrated from Mu‘adh ibn Anas who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "The verse of Might (آية العز) is: «*وَقُلِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي لَمْ يَتَّخِذْ وَلَداً*» the entire verse."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: regarding His saying, the Exalted: «*وَلَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُ وَلِيٌّ مِنَ الذُّلِ*». He said: "**لم يذل فيحتاج إلى ولي ينصره**" — "He was not humiliated such that He would need an ally to help Him."

## "Another Traditionary and Quranic Research"

Related to His saying, the Exalted: «*وَقُرْآناً فَرَقْناهُ لِتَقْرَأَهُ عَلَى النَّاسِ عَلى مُكْثٍ*» in three sections:

1 — The Holy Quran has parts by which it is known, such as the *juz’*, the *hizb*, the *‘ushr*, and others. Those whose consideration traces back to a specific attention from the Mighty Book itself are two: the Surah and the Ayah (verse). Allah, the Glorified, has repeatedly mentioned them in His speech, such as His saying: «*سُورَةٌ أَنْزَلْناها*» — **‘[This is] a surah which We have sent down’** (24:1) and His saying: «*قُلْ فَأْتُوا بِسُورَةٍ مِثْلِهِ*» — **‘Say, ‘Then bring a surah like it’’** (10:38), and others.

Their usage has been frequent on the tongue of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), the companions, and the Imams—a frequency that leaves no doubt that they have a reality in the Holy Quran. A Surah is a collection of divine speech beginning with the *basmalah*, aimed at explaining a purpose; this purpose identifies the Surah consistently and without exception, except for *Bara’ah* (Surah al-Tawbah). It has been narrated from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام) that it consists of verses from Surah al-Anfal. Other exceptions include what has been narrated from them (عليه‌السلام) that *al-Duha* and *Alam Nashrah* are one Surah, and that *al-Fil* and *al-Ilaf* are one Surah.

Similar is the case for the Ayah (verse). The application of "Ayah" to a segment of speech has been repeated in His speech, the Exalted, such as His saying: «*وَإِذا تُلِيَتْ عَلَيْهِمْ آياتُهُ زادَتْهُمْ إِيماناً*» — **‘and when His signs are recited to them they increase them in faith’** (8:2), and His saying: «*كِتابٌ فُصِّلَتْ آياتُهُ قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا*» — **‘a Book whose verses have been elaborated as an Arabic Quran’** (41:3). It has been narrated from Umm Salamah that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to stop at the ends of the verses. It is established that Surah al-Hamd is seven verses, and it is narrated from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) that Surah al-Mulk is thirty verses, among other things indicating that numbering was applied to the verses in the speech of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and his Progeny.

What reflection on the division of Arabic speech into segments and sections naturally provides—especially in rhymed speech—and then contemplation on what has been narrated from the Prophet and his Progeny (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding the number of verses, is that an Ayah of the Quran is a segment of speech upon which recitation should naturally rely by separating it from what precedes it and what follows it.

This varies according to the contexts, especially in rhymed contexts. Sometimes it is a single word, such as His saying: «*مُدْهامَّتانِ*» — **‘dark green’** (55:64). Sometimes it is two words or more, whether a complete sentence or not, such as His saying: «*الرَّحْمنُ عَلَّمَ الْقُرْآنَ خَلَقَ الْإِنْسانَ عَلَّمَهُ الْبَيانَ*» — **‘The All-beneficent has taught the Quran. He created man, [and] taught him articulate speech.’** (55:1-4) and His saying: «*الْحَاقَّةُ مَا الْحَاقَّةُ وَما أَدْراكَ مَا الْحَاقَّةُ*» — **‘The Inevitable! What is the Inevitable? And what will show you what the Inevitable is?’** (69:1-3). Sometimes it is long, like the Verse of Debt (آية الدين) in Surah al-Baqarah (2:282).

2 — As for the number of Quranic Surahs, they are one hundred and fourteen Surahs according to the standard arrangement in the *mus’haf* circulating among us, which corresponds to the Uthmanic *mus’haf*. The speech of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام) regarding this has preceded: that they do not count *Bara’ah* as an independent Surah, and they count *al-Duha* and *Alam Nashrah* as one Surah, and *al-Fil* and *al-Ilaf* as one Surah.

As for the number of verses (الآي), no *mutawatir* text has been transmitted that defines the verses and distinguishes each verse from others, nor is there any solitary report (الآحاد) that can be relied upon. The clearest evidence for this is the disagreement among the scholars of numbering (أهل العدد) among themselves: the Meccans, the Medinans, the Syrians, the Basrans, and the Kufans.

Some said the total of the Quran is six thousand verses. Others said six thousand two hundred and four verses. It was also said: and fourteen; and said: and nineteen; and said: and twenty-five; and said: and thirty-six.

The Meccans narrated their numbering from ‘Abdullah ibn Kathir from Mujahid from ibn ‘Abbas from Ubayy ibn Ka‘b. The Medinans have two numberings: one ending at Abu Ja‘far Marthad ibn al-Qa‘qa‘ and Shaybah ibn Nassah, and the other at Isma‘il ibn Ja‘far ibn Abi Kathir al-Ansari. The people of Syria narrated their numbering from Abu al-Darda’. The numbering of the people of Basra ends at ‘Asim ibn al-‘Ajjaj al-Jahdari. The numbering of the people of Kufa is attributed to Hamzah, al-Kisa’i, and Khalaf. Hamzah said: "Ibn Abi Layla informed us of this numbering from Abu ‘Abd al-Rahman al-Sulami from ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib."

In short, since the numbers do not end at a *mutawatir* text or a single report of significance that can be relied upon to distinguish each verse from its sister, there is no obligation to adopt any of them. Whatever among them is clear and manifest is accepted; otherwise, the contemplating researcher may choose what his investigation leads him to.

That which is narrated from ‘Ali (عليه‌السلام) regarding the Kufan numbering is contradicted by the fact that the *basmalah* is not counted in any of the Surahs except for *Fatihat al-Kitab* as one of its verses, despite what is narrated from him (عليه‌السلام) and from other Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام) that the *basmalah* is a verse of the Quran and is part of every Surah that opens with it. This would necessitate an increase in the count by the number of *basmalahs*.

This is what deterred us from presenting the details of the numbering they mentioned here, and from mentioning the Surahs whose verse count they agreed upon—which are forty Surahs—and those whose count or verse-ends they disagreed upon—which are seventy-four Surahs—as well as what they agreed upon as being a complete verse or not being a verse, such as «*الر*» wherever it occurs in the Quran, and what was disagreed upon. Whoever wishes to see the details of that should refer to the relevant sources.

3 — Regarding the chronological order of the Surahs' revelation: It is narrated in *al-Itqan* from ibn al-Durays in *Fada’il al-Quran*, who said: Muhammad ibn ‘Abdullah ibn Abi Ja‘far al-Razi narrated to us, ‘Amr ibn Harun informed us, ‘Uthman ibn ‘Ata’ al-Khorasani narrated to us from his father from ibn ‘Abbas who said: "When the opening of a Surah was sent down in Mecca, it was recorded in Mecca, then Allah would add to it whatever He willed.

The first to be sent down of the Quran was *Iqra’ bi-ismi Rabbik*, then *Nun*, then *Ya ayyuha al-Muzzammil*, then *Ya ayyuha al-Muddaththir*, then *Tabbat yada Abi Lahab*, then *Idha al-shamsu kuwwirat*, then *Sabbih isma Rabbika al-A‘la*, then *Wa al-layli idha yaghsha*, then *Wa al-fajr*, then *Wa al-duha*, then *Alam nashrah*, then *Wa al-‘asr*, then *Wa al-‘adiyat*, then *Inna a‘taynaka*, then *Alhakum al-takathur*, then *Ara’ayta al-ladhi yukadhdhib*, then *Qul ya ayyuha al-kafirun*, then *Alam tara kayfa fa‘ala Rabbuk*, then *Qul a‘udhu bi-Rabbi al-falaq*, then *Qul a‘udhu bi-Rabbi al-nas*, then *Qul huwa Allahu ahad*, then *Wa al-najm*, then *‘Abasa*, then *Inna anzalnahu fi laylat al-qadr*, then *Wa al-shamsi wa duhaha*, then *Wa al-sama’i dhat al-buruj*, then *Wa al-tin*, then *Li-ilafi Quraysh*, then *Al-qari‘ah*, then *La uqsimu bi-yawm al-qiyamah*, then *Waylun li-kulli humazah*, then *Wa al-mursalat*, then *Qaf*, then *La uqsimu bi-hadha al-balad*, then *Wa al-sama’i wa al-tariq*, then *Iqtarabat al-sa‘ah*, then *Sad*, then *al-A‘raf*, then *Qul uhiya*, then *Ya-Sin*, then *al-Furqan*, then *al-Mala’ikah*, then *Kaf-Ha-Ya-‘Ayn-Sad*, then *Ta-Ha*, then *al-Waqi‘ah*, then *Ta-Sin-Mim al-Shu‘ara’*, then *Ta-Sin*, then *al-Qasas*, then *Bani Isra’il*, then *Yunus*, then *Hud*, then *Yusuf*, then *al-Hijr*, then *al-An‘am*, then *al-Saffat*, then *Luqman*, then *Saba’*, then *al-Zumar*, then *Ha-Mim al-Mu’min*, then *Ha-Mim al-Sajdah*, then *Ha-Mim-‘Ayn-Sin-Qaf*, then *Ha-Mim al-Zukhruf*, then *al-Dukhan*, then *al-Jathiyah*, then *al-Ahqaf*, then *al-Dhariyat*, then *al-Ghashiyah*, then *al-Kahf*, then *al-Nahl*, then *Inna arsalna Nuhan*, then Surah *Ibrahim*, then *al-Anbiya’*, then *al-Mu’minun*, then *Tanzil al-Sajdah*, then *al-Tur*, then *Tabarak al-Mulk*, then *al-Haqqah*, then *Sa’ala*, then *‘Amma yatasa’alun*, then *al-Nazi‘at*, then *Idha al-sama’u infatarat*, then *Idha al-sama’u inshaqqat*, then *al-Rum*, then *al-‘Ankabut*, then *Waylun lil-mutaffifin*. This is what Allah sent down in Mecca.

Then Allah sent down in Medina Surah *al-Baqarah*, then *al-Anfal*, then *Al ‘Imran*, then *al-Ahzab*, then *al-Mumtahanah*, then *al-Nisa’*, then *Idha zulzilat*, then *al-Hadid*, then *al-Qital*, then *al-Ra‘d*, then *al-Rahman*, then *al-Insan*, then *al-Talaq*, then *Lam yakun*, then *al-Hashr*, then *Idha ja’a nasru Allah*, then *al-Nur*, then *al-Hajj*, then *al-Munafiqun*, then *al-Mujadilah*, then *al-Hujurat*, then *al-Tahrim*, then *al-Jumu‘ah*, then *al-Taghabun*, then *al-Saff*, then *al-Fath*, then *al-Ma’idah*, then *Bara’ah*."

Surah *Fatihat al-Kitab* fell out of the narration; it is sometimes said that it was sent down twice, once in Mecca and once in Medina.

It is also narrated therein from al-Bayhaqi in *Dala’il al-Nubuwwah*, that he narrated with his chain from ‘Ikrimah and al-Husayn ibn Abi al-Hasan, who both said: "Allah sent down of the Quran in Mecca *Iqra’ bi-ismi Rabbik*..." and they recounted the tradition similar to the previous tradition of ‘Ata’ from ibn ‘Abbas, except that *al-Fatihah*, *al-A‘raf*, and *Kaf-Ha-Ya-‘Ayn-Sad* fell out of what was sent down in Mecca.

Also, it mentioned therein *Ha-Mim al-Dukhan* before *Ha-Mim al-Sajdah*, then *Idha al-sama’u inshaqqat* before *Idha al-sama’u infatarat*, then *Waylun lil-mutaffifin* before *al-Baqarah* among what was sent down in Medina, then *Al ‘Imran* before *al-Anfal*, then *al-Ma’idah* before *al-Mumtahanah*.

Then al-Bayhaqi narrated with his chain from Mujahid from ibn ‘Abbas that he said: "The first that Allah sent down upon His Prophet of the Quran was *Iqra’ bi-ismi Rabbik*..." the tradition, and it corresponds to the tradition of ‘Ikrimah in its order, and it mentioned the Surahs that fell out of ‘Ikrimah's tradition among what was sent down in Mecca.

In it, from the book *al-Nasikh wa al-Mansukh* by ibn Hissar: "The Medinan [Surahs] by consensus are twenty Surahs, the disagreed upon are twelve Surahs, and everything else is Meccan by consensus." [End of quote].

What they agreed upon as Medinan are: *al-Baqarah*, *Al ‘Imran*, *al-Nisa’*, *al-Ma’idah*, *al-Anfal*, *al-Tawbah*, *al-Nur*, *al-Ahzab*, Surah *Muhammad*, *al-Fath*, *al-Hujurat*, *al-Hadid*, *al-Mujadilah*, *al-Hashr*, *al-Mumtahanah*, *al-Munafiqun*, *al-Jumu‘ah*, *al-Talaq*, *al-Tahrim*, and *al-Nasr*.

What they disagreed upon regarding its Meccan or Medinan status are: Surah *al-Ra‘d*, *al-Rahman*, *al-Jinn*, *al-Saff*, *al-Taghabun*, *al-Mutaffifin*, *al-Qadr*, *al-Bayyinah*, *al-Zilzal*, *al-Tawhid*, and the *Mu‘awwidhatayn* (the two Surahs of seeking refuge).

Knowledge of the Meccan and Medinan status of the Surahs, and then the order of their revelation, has a significant impact on research related to the prophetic mission (الدعوة النبوية) and its spiritual, political, and civil course during his time (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and the analysis of his noble biography (السيرة). The narrations—as you see—are not fit to stand as a relied-upon proof in establishing any of that, especially since the contradictions among them strip them of credibility.

Thus, the determined path for this purpose is contemplation (التدبر) on the context of the verses and seeking aid from the indications and internal and external signs that are obtained. We shall proceed upon this in this book, and Allah is the One whose help is sought.
